title: 24-Hour Cafés
context:
One of the things that surprises many first-time visitors to South Korea is that cafés remain busy even late at night. While cafés in many countries close during the evening, it is common in Korea for cafés to stay open until midnight or even operate twenty-four hours a day.

They are not simply places to drink coffee but have become important social spaces where people study, work, read, or spend time with friends.

This café culture grew rapidly as Korean cities became more urbanized and apartment living became common. Since many people have limited personal space at home, cafés provide a comfortable environment where they can relax for long periods.

Students often prepare for exams there, office workers finish projects after work, and friends gather to talk well into the night. Because of this, it is normal for someone to remain in a café for several hours after ordering a single drink.

Visitors may notice that many customers speak quietly or use headphones, creating a calm atmosphere despite the café being full. Before leaving, customers are generally expected to return their cups and trays to the designated return area to help keep the café clean for the next guests.

For Koreans, cafés are much more than businesses that serve coffee. They have become an important part of everyday life, offering a place to study, socialize, or simply enjoy some quiet time. 

Understanding this culture helps visitors appreciate why cafés play such a significant role in modern Korean society.

title: Jjimjilbang (Korean Bathhouses)
context:
One of the most distinctive experiences in South Korea is visiting a jjimjilbang, a traditional Korean bathhouse. Unlike an ordinary public bath, a jjimjilbang is a place where people can bathe, relax, eat, sleep, and spend time with family or friends. 

Many are open twenty-four hours a day, making them a popular destination not only for relaxation but also for socializing and resting.Before entering the bathing area, visitors are expected to shower thoroughly. 

The baths are shared by everyone, so washing beforehand is considered an important sign of respect for other guests. The bathing areas are separated by gender, and bathing is done without swimsuits. 

While this may feel unfamiliar to first-time visitors, Koreans generally view it as a natural part of the experience rather than something embarrassing. Speaking quietly, avoiding splashing, and keeping the baths clean are also considered good manners.

After bathing, people change into the comfortable clothing provided by the bathhouse and move to the common areas, where families and friends gather together. Many people relax in heated sauna rooms made from materials such as salt, clay, charcoal, or jade, each believed to provide different health benefits. 

It is also common to enjoy traditional snacks such as sikhye (a sweet rice drink) and baked eggs, which have become closely associated with the jjimjilbang experience.For many Koreans, a jjimjilbang is much more than a place to wash. It is a space to relieve stress, spend quality time with loved ones, and take a break from daily life. 

By understanding its customs and etiquette, visitors can experience one of the most familiar and comforting traditions of Korean culture.

title: K-pop Culture
context:
One of the most recognizable aspects of modern South Korea is K-pop, a music genre that has grown into a global cultural phenomenon. 

Although Korean popular music has existed for decades, modern K-pop began developing in the 1990s by combining pop, hip-hop, R&B, and electronic music with highly synchronized dance performances and carefully planned stage productions. Today, it has become one of Korea's most influential cultural exports.

For many Koreans, K-pop is more than entertainment. It reflects creativity, teamwork, and dedication. Idols often spend many years as trainees, practicing singing, dancing, languages, and public speaking before making their debut. 

This long period of preparation is admired as a symbol of hard work and perseverance.Visitors to Korea will often hear K-pop music in cafés, shopping streets, convenience stores, and public events. Many fans also visit entertainment company buildings, themed cafés, and shops selling official merchandise. 

However, fans are expected to respect artists' privacy by avoiding their homes or private schedules.K-pop has also introduced many people around the world to Korean language, fashion, food, and culture. As international interest has grown, it has encouraged cultural exchange and increased tourism to Korea.

Understanding K-pop helps visitors see how music has become an important part of Korea's modern identity, connecting traditional values of discipline and effort with contemporary creativity and global influence.

title: Late-Night Food Culture
context:
One of the most enjoyable parts of life in South Korea is its vibrant late-night food culture. Even after midnight, it is common to see restaurants, street food stalls, and convenience stores filled with people enjoying a meal. 

For many Koreans, eating late at night is not unusual but a natural part of daily life, especially after work, studying, or spending time with friends.Sharing food is an important aspect of Korean culture. Rather than eating alone, people often gather to enjoy dishes such as fried chicken, spicy rice cakes, noodles, or soups while talking about their day. 

This tradition reflects the value Koreans place on spending time together and strengthening relationships through food.Visitors may also notice that food delivery services operate until very late, and in many cities it is possible to order meals almost any time of the day. 

This convenience has become a defining feature of modern Korean life.When dining with others, it is considered polite to wait until everyone has received their food before beginning to eat. Meals are usually shared, so taking only your portion and being considerate of others are important manners.

Korea's late-night food culture is more than simply eating after dark. It provides an opportunity for people to relax, celebrate, and connect with family, friends, or colleagues. 

By experiencing this tradition, visitors gain a better understanding of the warmth and social nature of everyday Korean life.

title: BBQ culture
context:
One of the most well-known dining experiences in South Korea is Korean barbecue, where diners cook meat on a grill placed in the center of the table. Unlike restaurants where food is prepared entirely in the kitchen, Korean barbecue encourages everyone at the table to participate in cooking and sharing the meal, making it a social experience rather than simply eating.

Sharing food has long been an important part of Korean culture, and Korean barbecue reflects this tradition. Family members, friends, and colleagues often gather around the grill, talking while taking turns cooking the meat. 

The meal is usually accompanied by banchan, a variety of small side dishes that are shared by everyone. Wrapping grilled meat in lettuce with garlic, sauce, and other ingredients is also a common way to enjoy the meal.

Visitors may notice that the oldest person or the host often begins cooking or serves others first. Accepting food politely and waiting for everyone before eating are considered good manners. 

Since the grill is shared, people are also expected to avoid taking more than their share and to be considerate of others at the table.For Koreans, barbecue is more than a popular dish. 

It is an opportunity to strengthen relationships, celebrate special occasions, or simply spend time together after work or on weekends. 

Understanding this tradition helps visitors appreciate how food in Korea is closely connected with hospitality, community, and everyday social life.

title: Cosmetics Shopping
context:
One of the first things many visitors notice in South Korea is the large number of cosmetics stores found in shopping districts. 

Korean skincare and beauty products have become popular around the world, but for Koreans, skincare is often considered a part of daily self-care rather than something reserved for special occasions.Korean beauty culture places great importance on maintaining healthy, well-hydrated skin. 

Instead of using makeup to hide imperfections, many people focus on building a consistent skincare routine to keep their skin in good condition. As a result, products such as cleansers, toners, serums, and sunscreens are commonly used by both women and men. 

Taking care of one's appearance is generally seen as a sign of cleanliness, confidence, and respect for oneself and others.Visitors may also notice that cosmetics stores often allow customers to freely test products before making a purchase. 

Staff members usually provide samples or demonstrate how products are used, making shopping a more interactive experience.Although Korean beauty standards continue to evolve, skincare remains deeply connected to everyday life. 

It is common for people of different ages to discuss skincare products, exchange recommendations, or give cosmetics as gifts to family and friends.

By understanding Korea's skincare culture, visitors can see that cosmetics are not simply fashion items but an important part of daily routines and modern Korean lifestyle.

title: Palace Hanbok Rentals
context:
One of the most memorable cultural experiences in South Korea is wearing a hanbok, the traditional Korean dress, while visiting historic palaces. Although hanbok is no longer everyday clothing, it continues to be worn during important occasions such as weddings, traditional holidays, and cultural celebrations. 

In recent years, renting a hanbok to explore royal palaces has become a popular way for both locals and visitors to connect with Korea's history.The hanbok is known for its elegant design, bright colors, and comfortable shape. 

Traditionally, its colors and patterns could represent a person's age, social status, or the occasion being celebrated. Wearing a hanbok while walking through historic sites allows people to imagine what life may have been like during Korea's royal dynasties.

When wearing a hanbok, visitors are encouraged to treat both the clothing and the historical sites with respect. Running, sitting carelessly on the garment, or behaving loudly is generally discouraged. 

Many people also take photographs, but they are expected to avoid disturbing others who are enjoying the same experience.For many Koreans, wearing a hanbok is more than dressing in traditional clothing. It is a way of preserving cultural heritage and expressing pride in the country's history. 

By experiencing this tradition, visitors gain a deeper appreciation of Korea's customs and the lasting importance of its historical identity.

title: Efficient Subway
context:
One of the first things many visitors notice in South Korea is the efficiency of its subway system. In large cities, the subway is one of the most common ways to travel because it is fast, reliable, and connects residential areas, business districts, shopping streets, and tourist attractions. 

For many Koreans, using the subway is simply part of everyday life.The subway is also known for its orderly atmosphere. Passengers usually stand in lines while waiting for the train and allow people to get off before boarding. 

Inside the train, it is common for people to speak quietly, read, listen to music with headphones, or rest during their journey. This creates a calm environment even during busy commuting hours.Visitors may notice seats marked for elderly passengers, pregnant women, or people with disabilities. 

Even when these seats are empty, many people avoid using them out of respect for those who may need them later. Eating strong-smelling food and speaking loudly on the phone are also generally considered impolite.

The subway reflects important values in Korean society, such as consideration for others, punctuality, and respect for shared public spaces. 

By following these customs, visitors can experience not only an efficient transportation system but also the everyday etiquette that helps millions of people travel comfortably. 

Understanding these small habits provides valuable insight into the discipline and mutual respect that are important parts of modern Korean culture.

title: Street Food Markets
context:
One of the liveliest parts of visiting South Korea is exploring its street food markets. These markets are more than places to eat—they are important community spaces where people gather to shop, meet friends, and enjoy local specialties.

 Markets have been part of Korean life for centuries, serving as centers of trade and social interaction.Visitors will often find a wide variety of freshly prepared foods, including tteokbokki (spicy rice cakes), hotteok (sweet pancakes), gimbap, fish cakes, and grilled snacks. Many dishes are cooked in front of customers, allowing people to watch the preparation while enjoying the lively atmosphere.

 It is common for locals to stop by after work or while shopping to enjoy a quick meal or snack. When visiting a street food market, people are expected to be considerate of others by waiting patiently, keeping the area clean, and returning trays or dishes when requested.

 Since many stalls have limited space, customers usually finish eating before moving on to the next stall.Street food markets represent the warmth and energy of Korean daily life. 

They bring together people of all ages and backgrounds, creating a welcoming atmosphere where food is shared and conversations naturally begin. 

By visiting these markets, travelers experience not only the flavors of Korea but also the sense of community and hospitality that has been an important part of Korean culture for generations.

title: Reason why Japanese wash their body before getting into a hot spring
context:
One of the things that surprises foreign visitors to Japanese hot springs for the first time is the rule that you must always wash your body before entering the bath. Hot spring baths are not considered a place for washing, but rather a place to soak in warm water and relax.

 Therefore, to ensure that all visitors can enjoy the hot springs comfortably, visitors are expected to wash off any dirt beforehand in the showers or washing areas.

 Furthermore, it is considered good etiquette not to bring towels into the bath or to make a loud commotion. Hot springs are part of Japan’s traditional culture and are cherished as a special space for healing both mind and body. By understanding these rules, you will be able to experience Japanese hot spring culture more comfortably.

title: Why its quite inside Japanese trains
context:
Foreign visitors may be surprised to find that most people on Japanese trains keep to themselves. As many people use them for commuting to work or school, it is considered important not to cause a disturbance to those around you.

 People refrain from making phone calls, and conversations are kept at a relatively low volume. Furthermore, as many people sleep or read on the trains, a quiet atmosphere is naturally maintained.

 These manners are not enforced by law, but are upheld by people’s consideration for others and their sense of public responsibility. Understanding Japanese train culture will help you enjoy a more comfortable journey.

title: Why are Japanese convenience stores so convenient?
context:
Convenience stores in Japan are more than just places to shop. As well as selling drinks and packed lunches, they offer a wide range of services, such as paying utility bills, sending parcels, purchasing tickets and using ATMs.

 A key feature is that many shops are open 24 hours a day, meaning they can be used whenever needed. Furthermore, the quality of the products is high, and new items are frequently introduced. Consequently, it is not uncommon for foreign travellers to visit convenience stores almost every day.

 It is fair to say that they play a vital role in supporting daily life in Japan.

title: What is the difference between a Shinto shrine and a Buddhist temple?
context:
There are numerous shrines and temples in Japan, but there are quite a few foreigners who are unaware of the difference between them. 

Shrines are Shinto places of worship, characterised by the presence of torii gates. Temples, on the other hand, are Buddhist places of worship and typically feature Buddhist statues and graves. 

There are also differences in the manner of worship: at shrines, the customary practice is to bow twice, clap twice and bow once, whilst at temples, people usually pray quietly with their hands pressed together. 

Although many Japanese people do not have a strong sense of belonging to any particular religion, they visit shrines and temples to mark important life events and occasions. 

This unique religious culture is one of the fascinating aspects of understanding Japan.

title: Why don’t people give tips in Japan?
context:
Whilst it is customary to tip in restaurants and hotels in many countries, this is not the norm in Japan. In Japan, providing good service is considered part of the job, so there is no need to give extra money. 

In fact, if you do try to give a tip, staff may be taken aback or even attempt to return it. Where a service charge is applicable, it is often included in the price from the outset, so customers need only pay the amount shown on the bill. 

Being aware of this cultural difference can help minimise any confusion whilst travelling in Japan.

title: Why do we need bathroom slippers?
context:
In Japanese homes and ryokans, slippers specifically for the toilet are sometimes provided. This is to distinguish the toilet floor from the living area and to maintain cleanliness. 

When entering the toilet, one should change into these slippers, and when leaving, put one’s own slippers back on. Some foreign visitors forget to change their slippers and walk out as they are, but in Japan this is regarded as a slightly embarrassing slip-up. 

This custom reflects the Japanese people’s high standards of cleanliness and is one of the cultural practices deeply rooted in everyday life.

title: Why Japanese People Bow
context:
Bowing is one of the cultural practices that symbolises Japan. It is used to greet others, express gratitude, apologise or show respect, and the angle and duration of the bow vary depending on the situation. 

There are various types of bows, ranging from a slight nod to a deep bow, and it is an important way of expressing respect for others. 

It is regarded as particularly important in business settings, where it helps to smooth relations with people you are meeting for the first time. 

Whilst it may seem a little difficult for foreigners, even a slight bow as a basic greeting can make a good impression.

title: Cherry blossoms hold special significance in Japan
context:
Cherry blossoms are known throughout the world as Japan’s national flower. However, for the Japanese, cherry blossoms are not merely beautiful flowers. 

They symbolise the arrival of spring and new beginnings, and coincide with the season of school and company induction ceremonies. 

Furthermore, as they fall within a short period, they are also regarded as a symbol of the transience and beauty of life. Every spring, many people enjoy ‘hanami’ (cherry blossom viewing), savouring meals and conversation whilst admiring the blossoms in parks and along riverbanks. 

This special affection for cherry blossoms is an essential element in understanding Japanese culture.

title: Why are there so many vending machines in Japan?
context:
There are millions of vending machines installed across Japan; they can be found in town centres, at stations, in residential areas and even in mountainous regions. 

Some machines sell not only drinks but also ice cream and food. One reason for the widespread use of vending machines is the country’s high level of public safety. 

As the risk of vandalism or theft is relatively low, they can be installed in many locations. Furthermore, their convenience—being available 24 hours a day—is another reason for their popularity. 

For foreign travellers, Japan’s vending machine culture is sure to be a fascinating experience.

title: Why do we carry out a big end-of-year clean?
context:
In Japan, as the end of the year approaches, people carry out a special cleaning ritual known as ‘o-seki’. This is not merely a matter of tidying up; it is a traditional custom designed to rid the home of a year’s worth of grime and misfortune, so that the New Year can be welcomed with a fresh start. 

O-seki is not confined to the home; it is also sometimes carried out in schools and workplaces. Windows, floors and even areas that are not usually cleaned are scrubbed thoroughly to prepare for the New Year. 

This custom embodies not only physical cleanliness but also the idea of renewing one’s spirit. For the Japanese, the New Year is a special occasion, and o-seki is one of the key preparations for it.

title: National parks
context:
One of the defining features of the United States is its extensive network of national parks, created to protect the country's diverse natural landscapes. 

From mountains and forests to deserts and canyons, these parks preserve places of exceptional beauty and provide opportunities for people to experience nature.For many Americans, visiting national parks is a family tradition. 

During weekends or holidays, people enjoy hiking, camping, wildlife watching, and scenic drives. Spending time outdoors is often seen as a way to relax, stay active, and appreciate the natural environment.Visitors are expected to protect the parks by staying on marked trails, disposing of waste properly, and avoiding disturbing wildlife. 

These rules help preserve the parks for future generations.National parks are more than tourist attractions. 

They reflect the American belief that nature is an important part of the country's heritage and should be carefully protected.

title: Road trips
context:
One of the most iconic travel experiences in the United States is the road trip. Because the country covers a vast area with diverse landscapes, traveling by car has become an important part of American culture. 

Improvements in highways during the 20th century made long-distance driving easier, encouraging families and friends to explore different parts of the country. For many Americans, a road trip is more than simply reaching a destination. 

It is an opportunity to enjoy changing scenery, visit small towns, stop at local attractions, and spend quality time together. During school vacations or holidays, many families travel by car instead of flying, creating memories through the journey itself.

Road trips also reflect the value Americans place on independence and personal freedom. Travelers can choose their own routes, make spontaneous stops, and explore places at their own pace. Along the way, they often visit scenic viewpoints, roadside diners, or national parks.

The tradition of road trips has become a symbol of exploration and adventure in the United States.

title: Diverse regional cultures
context:
The most distinctive feature of the United States is its diverse regional cultures. As a large country shaped by immigration and local history, different regions have developed their own traditions, accents, foods, festivals, and ways of life. 

Traveling from one state to another can sometimes feel like visiting a different country.For example, the southern states are known for their hospitality and traditional comfort foods, while the northeastern states are recognized for their historical landmarks and older cities. 

The western states are often associated with national parks and outdoor lifestyles, whereas the Midwest is known for its agricultural heritage and strong sense of community. Each region has its own customs that reflect its history and environment.

Americans often take pride in their regional identity while also sharing a common national identity. Local festivals, sports teams, and traditional dishes are important ways people celebrate their community and heritage.

The United States is a collection of many local cultures that have developed over time. This diversity is one of the country's greatest strengths and is an important part of its national identity.

title: Diners
context:
One of the most recognizable symbols of everyday life in the United States is the diner. Originally becoming popular in the early 20th century, diners were designed as simple, affordable restaurants where people could enjoy a meal at almost any time of day. 

Today, they remain an important part of American culture, especially in small towns and along highways.Unlike many restaurants that specialize in a particular meal, diners usually serve breakfast, lunch, and dinner throughout the day. 

It is common for people to order pancakes in the evening or a hamburger in the morning. Families, travelers, students, and workers all gather in the same space, making diners welcoming places for people from different backgrounds.

Visitors may notice that friendly conversation between customers and staff is common. Regular customers often know the employees by name, creating a warm and familiar atmosphere. Since table service is provided, leaving a tip for the server is considered customary.

Diners are more than places to eat. They represent hospitality, community, and the relaxed nature of everyday American life.

title: Tipping culture
context:
Unlike countries where service charges are included in the price, it is customary in the United States to leave a tip for workers in restaurants and certain service industries. This practice became common over time as tips became an important part of many workers' income.

For Americans, leaving a tip is generally seen as a way of showing appreciation for good service rather than simply giving extra money. Restaurant servers, hotel staff, taxi drivers, and other service workers often expect tips as part of their earnings. 

Because of this, many Americans automatically include a tip when paying for a service.Visitors who are unfamiliar with this custom may accidentally leave without tipping, but this can be considered impolite in situations where tipping is expected. 

Learning when and where to tip helps visitors avoid misunderstandings and shows respect for local customs. 

Although it may feel unusual to visitors from countries where tipping is uncommon, it has become an established social practice and remains an important part of American service culture.

title: Huge Shopping Malls
context:
As suburban communities expanded during the 20th century, shopping malls became popular places where people could shop, dine, and spend time together in a single location. Many malls include hundreds of stores, restaurants, cinemas, and entertainment facilities under one roof.

For many Americans, visiting a shopping mall is more than simply buying products. Families and friends often meet there on weekends to eat, watch movies, or spend time together. 

Teenagers also gather at malls as places to socialize, making them important community spaces in many towns and cities.Unlike traditional markets, malls provide a comfortable indoor environment where people can spend several hours regardless of the weather. 

Seasonal decorations, holiday events, and special promotions also attract large numbers of visitors throughout the year. Although online shopping has become increasingly popular, shopping malls continue to play an important role in American culture. 

They represent convenience, consumer culture, and social interaction. Malls are not only commercial centers but also places where many Americans relax, meet others, and enjoy everyday leisure activities.

title: Sports Culture
context:
Sports play an important role in everyday life in the United States. Many Americans grow up playing sports at school, supporting local teams, or watching professional competitions with family and friends. 

Popular sports such as American football, baseball, basketball, and ice hockey are not only forms of entertainment but also important parts of local and national identity.Supporting a sports team often creates a strong sense of community. 

On game days, fans wear their team's colors, gather at stadiums, restaurants, or homes, and celebrate victories together. School and university sports are also highly valued, with many communities actively supporting their local teams.

Visitors may notice that sporting events include traditions beyond the game itself, such as singing the national anthem before the match, cheering together, and enjoying food during the event. 

Fans are encouraged to support their team enthusiastically while showing respect for players, officials, and opposing supporters.For many Americans, sports are more than competition. They teach teamwork, perseverance, and school spirit while bringing people together across different backgrounds. 

America's sports culture helps visitors appreciate how athletic events strengthen communities and create shared experiences that become lasting memories for many families.

title: 24-hour Cities like Las Vegas
context:
One feature that many visitors notice in parts of the United States is the presence of 24-hour cities, where entertainment, restaurants, and other services continue throughout the night. 

Although most American cities become quieter after midnight, places such as Las Vegas are famous for their lively atmosphere at all hours.

The city's entertainment industry developed around the idea that visitors could enjoy shows, restaurants, shopping, and other attractions both day and night. As a result, many businesses operate around the clock to serve tourists and local workers whose schedules differ from the traditional daytime routine.

Visitors may notice that the streets remain busy late into the night, with people enjoying concerts, live performances, and other forms of entertainment. 

Although the atmosphere is energetic, it is still important to respect public rules, follow local laws, and be considerate of others.

Twenty-four-hour cities represent a different side of American culture, where convenience and entertainment are available almost any time of day. 

While many communities value quiet evenings, some cities have built their identity around being active and welcoming throughout the entire day and night.
